name: Myanmar civil war (post-2021 coup)
summary: Myanmar’s post-2021 coup civil war began after the Tatmadaw overthrew the elected government on 1 February 2021, violently suppressed protests, and triggered nationwide armed resistance by PDFs, local defense forces, and ethnic armed organizations. By January 2026, ACLED recorded nearly 93,000 fatalities, but public data do not cleanly split these deaths by side or civilian/combatant status. The junta is responsible for the overwhelming majority of verified direct civilian killings, while anti-junta forces have also killed civilians, especially alleged collaborators and, in Rakhine, Rohingya civilians.

sides.name: Myanmar military junta / State Administration Council

military_killed.notes: No independently verified cumulative 2021-2026 junta combatant death total was found. The NUG Ministry of Defence claimed 14,093 junta/security deaths and 7,363 injured in 2024 military reporting, but this is a partisan claim and was criticized as methodologically opaque.

breakdown.notes: OHCHR verified 982 civilian deaths from airstrikes in 2025 alone, including 232 children and 368 women. ACLED recorded more than 6,300 military air and drone strikes causing at least 4,700 fatalities from 1 February 2021 to 23 January 2026, but ACLED’s figure is not civilian-only.
ISP-Myanmar recorded 91 civilian massacres by junta forces killing 1,776 civilians from 1 February 2021 to 9 December 2025.
PRIO reports that AAPP identified 884 protest-related killings by security forces in the early post-coup period.
OHCHR verified 2,237 deaths in military custody since the coup; some of these deaths may overlap with direct-civilian-killing totals.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: OHCHR gives a minimum of 7,646 civilians killed at the hands of the military since the coup; AAPP reported 7,747 pro-democracy activists and civilians killed by the junta and pro-military groups as of 4 February 2026. True totals are higher because thousands of reported deaths remain unverified.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Verified deaths in military custody since the coup, caused by torture, denial of care, prison conditions, and related custody abuses; overlap with direct-killing figures is possible.

Deaths from aid denial, health collapse, food insecurity, and displacement are not credibly quantified. The UN estimates over 16 million people need life-saving humanitarian assistance/protection in 2026; WFP says more than 12 million face acute hunger and displacement may rise from 3.6 million to 4 million.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: rural civilians, especially in Sagaing and central Myanmar
deaths_from_actions.notes: Deaths from arson/destruction are not separately quantified. HRW reported more than 100,000 homes burned by the military from May 2021 to August 2024.

sides.name: Anti-junta forces
members: Karen National Union / Karen National Liberation Army
Chin National Front / Chin National Army
other ethnic armed organizations and allied resistance groups

military_killed.notes: SRD/Mizzima reported 5,005 revolutionary-force deaths from the coup to 18 January 2025, including 1,144 in 2024. This is a documented subset and likely excludes some EAO losses.

breakdown.victims_group: alleged informants, local administrators, USDP members, civil servants, religious personnel and other alleged collaborators
breakdown.notes: PRIO attributed 2,152 reported civilian killings to armed opponents of the coup from 1 February 2021 to 30 September 2022. No comparable nationwide post-2022 aggregate was found.

breakdown.victims_group: Rohingya civilians fleeing Maungdaw/Naf River area
breakdown.notes: HRW’s 2025 World Report states approximately 180 people were reportedly killed following drone strikes and shelling on civilians fleeing Maungdaw on 5 August 2024; survivors and Rohingya sources blamed the Arakan Army, which denied attacking civilians.

breakdown.cause: attacks on civilian officials and suspected sympathizers
breakdown.victims_group: administrators, election officials, family members, civilians, religious personnel
breakdown.notes: OHCHR reported at least 169 incidents claimed by anti-military groups in 2025 involving attacks on local administrators, family members, civilians and religious personnel, but did not publish a death total.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: No current comprehensive nationwide aggregate exists. The low estimate combines PRIO’s first-20-month figure with the separately reported 2024 Maungdaw/Naf River mass-casualty incident and is almost certainly incomplete.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Rohingya civilians in Buthidaung and Maungdaw, Rakhine State
deaths_from_actions.notes: Deaths from displacement and access restrictions are not separately quantified. HRW reported Arakan Army operations and arson displaced an estimated 70,000 people from Buthidaung in May 2024.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: youth and civilians in areas under EAO or anti-military armed-group control
deaths_from_actions.notes: OHCHR reported forced recruitment concerns by anti-military armed groups, especially in Kachin, Shan and Rakhine, but did not quantify deaths.

Anti-military armed groups reportedly used anti-personnel landmines and remote-detonated roadside bombs. Myanmar had 889 documented civilian casualties from landmines and explosive remnants in the first nine months of 2024, but public sources do not cleanly attribute deaths by side.

totals.notes: ACLED recorded nearly 93,000 fatalities from 1 February 2021 to 23 January 2026; this is the best all-fatalities benchmark but is not a clean roll-up from side-specific military/civilian buckets. The civilian_low of 9,978 is a conservative additive floor from OHCHR's 7,646 military-attributed civilian deaths plus at least 2,332 attributed to anti-junta actors; true civilian and indirect totals are higher. military_low includes only documented anti-junta combatant deaths because no independently verified junta combatant total was found.

key_atrocities.name: Hlaing Tharyar massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Military and police killed at least 65 people in Hlaing Tharyar on 14 March 2021.

key_atrocities.name: Bago massacre
key_atrocities.notes: Security forces killed at least 82 people in Bago on 9 April 2021.

key_atrocities.name: Moso / Hpruso Christmas Eve massacre
key_atrocities.notes: More than 30 people, including two Save the Children staff, were killed in Kayah State on Christmas Eve 2021.

key_atrocities.name: Let Yet Kone school attack
key_atrocities.notes: Military helicopters attacked a school and village, killing at least 13 people including seven children; UNICEF later cited 11 children killed.

key_atrocities.name: Hpakant / A Nang Pa concert airstrike
key_atrocities.victims_group: concertgoers, Kachin civilians, entertainers and KIO/KIA personnel
key_atrocities.notes: Military airstrikes killed as many as 80 people at a KIO anniversary concert in Hpakant.

key_atrocities.name: Pa Zi Gyi / Pazigyi airstrike
key_atrocities.notes: HRW found the military used a thermobaric/enhanced-blast munition in an attack that killed more than 160 people, including children.

key_atrocities.name: Buthidaung and Maungdaw Rohingya attacks
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Arakan Army and Myanmar military junta / allied Rohingya armed groups in related incidents
key_atrocities.notes: HRW reported atrocities by both junta forces and the Arakan Army in Rakhine; roughly 180 people were reportedly killed following drone strikes and shelling on civilians fleeing Maungdaw on 5 August 2024. The Arakan Army denied attacking civilians.

key_atrocities.name: Kyauk Ni Maw market airstrike
key_atrocities.victims_group: civilians in Ramree Township, Rakhine State
key_atrocities.notes: OHCHR reported 41 civilians killed, including 12 women and seven children, in a military bombing of a Muslim village market.

key_atrocities.name: Oe Htein Kwin school airstrike
key_atrocities.notes: OHCHR reported 22 students and two female teachers killed when a military aircraft bombed a high school during class hours.

key_atrocities.name: Mrauk-U hospital airstrike
key_atrocities.notes: OHCHR reported at least 33 civilians killed and over 70 injured when military planes struck Mrauk-U general hospital on 10 December 2025.

uncertainty_notes: The major uncertainty is not whether the toll is catastrophic, but how to allocate deaths by side and category. ACLED provides a high all-fatalities benchmark of nearly 93,000 through 23 January 2026, while OHCHR/AAPP provide verified junta-attributed civilian counts around 7,646-7,747. Anti-junta civilian killings are systematically counted only for the first 20 months by PRIO; later incidents are documented qualitatively or event-by-event. No credible nationwide indirect-death estimate exists despite massive displacement, hunger, health collapse and aid-denial risks.
